ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific walnut cake entry; 'cake food product' (FOODON_03301116) is the closest defensible broader class.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for walnut cake; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of walnut cake is one slice of approximately 80 g, as commonly portioned in cafes and home settings.

nutrient_source.title: Walnut Cake — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Walnut Cake (cr_id F02010020). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Cake Products — University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 67 (medium) is consistent with published values for butter-based sponge/layer cakes in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL. Walnut cake shares a similar flour-sugar-fat matrix with vanilla/butter cake entries (GI range 54–80). GL per serving calculated as (67 × 35.2 × 0.80) / 100 = 18.9 for an 80 g slice.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (21.33 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (0.86 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 199 mg/100 g is also borderline elevated for a baked good.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit walnut cake due to its high sugar content (21.33 g/100 g) and moderate sodium (199 mg/100 g), both of which contribute to elevated blood pressure through distinct mechanisms; if consumed, a single small slice (≤50 g) on an infrequent basis is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 21.33 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; GI of 67 (medium) combined with a glycaemic load of 18.9 per 80 g serving represents a significant postprandial glucose challenge; dietary fibre is only 0.86 g/100 g, providing negligible glycaemic buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Walnut cake is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high free sugar content (21.33 g/100 g), medium GI (67), and high glycaemic load per serving (18.9); substituting refined flour with almond or whole-grain flour and reducing added sugar would substantially improve its glycaemic profile.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is 31.88 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat approximately 10.2 g/100 g from butter; high sugar (21.33 g/100 g) further elevates triglycerides and LDL-C risk; energy density is 458 kcal/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Walnut cake carries a high cardiovascular concern due to its elevated saturated fat (~10.2 g/100 g from butter), high sugar (21.33 g/100 g), and energy density (458 kcal/100 g); individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional indulgence and limit portion size to one small slice (≤50 g) infrequently.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Walnut cake is a baked Western-style butter cake enriched with chopped walnuts. It is prepared by creaming butter and sugar, incorporating eggs, then folding in self-raising flour, milk, and roughly chopped walnuts before baking until golden. The result is a moist, dense crumb with a slightly nutty flavour and a tender texture from the butter-fat matrix. Walnuts contribute omega-3 alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), protein, and a pleasant crunch, but the dominant nutritional character of the cake is defined by its high fat (31.88 g/100 g), high sugar (21.33 g/100 g), and moderate-to-high energy density (458 kcal/100 g). Dietary fibre is low at 0.86 g/100 g. The glycaemic index is 67 (medium) with a glycaemic load of approximately 18.9 per 80 g slice, making it a significant postprandial glucose challenge. Clinically, walnut cake is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control. It is commonly served as a snack or dessert in cafes, bakeries, and home settings across Singapore and internationally. Allergens include gluten, eggs, milk, and tree nuts (walnuts).
common_user_queries: How many calories in a slice of walnut cake?
Is walnut cake high in sugar?
What is the GI of walnut cake?
Is walnut cake suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02010020) at confidence 0.95. The GI value of 67 is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database analogues for butter-based cakes. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 10.2 g/100 g based on typical butter-cake composition cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central data for similar products; this single estimated field does not reduce overall confidence to medium given the strong HPB anchor for all other values.
notes: Saturated fat (10.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Cake, white, prepared from recipe without frosting' (NDB 18101) and typical walnut-butter cake composition, as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item and detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data are not available in the HPB source as of 2026-04-16. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database butter-cake entries; no direct walnut cake GI measurement was identified in the approved GI source. GL per 80 g serving = (67 × 35.2 × 0.80) / 100 = 18.9. The input record listed 'plant_based' as a dietary tag; this has been removed as walnut cake contains butter, eggs, and milk and is not plant-based. Walnut cake is a Western / international food item and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) under the G6 multilingual compliance rules.
